coredata.dc:title: Aucklandia lappa Decne carbon dots guided chemiluminescence: A sensitive detection strategy for salicylic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Yujie
Yang, Xian
Tan, Xuan
Zhao, Yue
Cheng, Ke
Han, Hongping
Zhang, Luoyilan
Xue, Hao
Xiang, Jing
Zhang, Xue
Zheng, Hong
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel chemiluminescent system was developed utilizing carbon dots (CDs) for the sensitive detection of salicylic acid. P,Cl co-doped carbon dots (P, Cl-CDs) was synthesized using discarded Aucklandia lappa Decne leaves as precursor materials through a one-step hydrothermal method. The morphology, structure, elemental composition, and properties of P, Cl-CDs were characterized by various methods including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and X-ray diffraction (XRD). The synthesized P, Cl-CDs exhibited uniform particle size and excellent water solubility. It was observed that P, Cl-CDs could significantly enhance the chemiluminescence intensity of the acidic KMnO4 system by approximately 10 times. While salicylic acid could inhibit the acidic KMnO4 system sensitized by P, Cl-CDs. Based on this phenomenon, a new system for detecting salicylic acid was established. Under optimal experimental conditions, the concentration of salicylic acid displayed a linear relationship in the range of 1 to 100 μM, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 4.2 nM and a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.9 % (n = 11). The method was successfully applied to determine salicylic acid in aspirin tablets, yielding spike recoveries within the range of 96.9 %–104.3 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Dots
Chemiluminescence
KMnO4
Salicylic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: QY and CL intensity of CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Innovation and Central Nervous System Drug Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was funded by the Science and Technology Research Program of the Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (Grant No. KJZD-K202300504) and the Innovation and Central Nervous System Drug Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (Grant No. 230041-01SZ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the FI-CL system. V: injection Valve, PMT: photomultiplier tube.

(a) TEM image of P, Cl-CDs (Inset: size distribution histogram(right) and crystal lattice(left)), (b) XRD spectrum of CDs and P, Cl-CDs, (c) Raman spectrum, (d) UV–vis spectrum (Right inset: photographs of P, Cl-CD at visible light, middle inset: FL spectra of P, Cl-CDs excited at wavelengths of 310–420 nm, with increments of 10 nm(A → L)).

(a) XPS survey spectrum of CDs and P, Cl-CDs, (b) XPS high-resolution of Cl 2p of P, Cl-CDs, (c) XPS high-resolution of C 1 s, (d) XPS high-resolution of O 1 s, (e) XPS high-resolution of P 2p of P, Cl-CDs, (f) FT-IR spectrum of CDs and P, Cl-CDs.

(a) Linear relationship between CL intensity and SA concentration. (b) Tolerable concentration ration of various interfering substances on SA detection, X representing different ions. (c) Effect of different radical scavengers on CL intensity. (d) The Uv–vis spectrum of a KMnO4, b KMnO4 + H+, c KMnO4 + H++P, Cl-CDs, d KMnO4 + H++P, Cl-CDs + SA, e KMnO4 + SA.
Detection limits and linear ranges of different methods for Salicylic acid detection.
Determination of Salicylic acid in real sample.

head.ce:title: Aucklandia lappa Decne carbon dots guided chemiluminescence: A sensitive detection strategy for salicylic acid

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center for Biotechnology of Active Substances

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: P, Cl-CDs synthesis strategies and chemiluminescence emission mechanism.

In this study, a novel chemiluminescent system was developed utilizing carbon dots (CDs) for the sensitive detection of salicylic acid. P,Cl co-doped carbon dots (P, Cl-CDs) was synthesized using discarded Aucklandia lappa Decne leaves as precursor materials through a one-step hydrothermal method. The morphology, structure, elemental composition, and properties of P, Cl-CDs were characterized by various methods including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and X-ray diffraction (XRD). The synthesized P, Cl-CDs exhibited uniform particle size and excellent water solubility. It was observed that P, Cl-CDs could significantly enhance the chemiluminescence intensity of the acidic KMnO4 system by approximately 10 times. While salicylic acid could inhibit the acidic KMnO4 system sensitized by P, Cl-CDs. Based on this phenomenon, a new system for detecting salicylic acid was established. Under optimal experimental conditions, the concentration of salicylic acid displayed a linear relationship in the range of 1 to 100 μM, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 4.2 nM and a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.9 % (n = 11). The method was successfully applied to determine salicylic acid in aspirin tablets, yielding spike recoveries within the range of 96.9 %–104.3 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Salicylic acid (SA) is a β-hydroxy acid renowned for its antimicrobial, preservative, and antiviral properties. It serves as a precursor for the synthesizing drugs like aspirin, mesalazine, olsalazine, and sodium salicylate [1]. Additionally, SA plays a pivotal role as a plant growth regulator, influencing growth, development, and response to environmental stress in plants [2]. Widely utilized for its anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and blood sugar-lowering properties in treating digestive and cardiovascular diseases, SA also functions as a preservative and antibacterial agent in cosmetics and personal care products. Despite these benefits, SA can irritate the esophagus, gastrointestinal mucosa, and skin, and excessive intake may lead to toxicity. Therefore, some countries do not allow the use of SA as a food additive, imposing strict limits in cosmetics and personal care products [3]. Excessive SA intake can cause poisoning symptoms like nausea, vomiting, coma, and metabolic abnormalities, with severe cases potentially resulting in death.

Hence, the detection of SA is of paramount importance. Various methods for detecting SA have been established, including High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) [4], Liquid Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (LC-MS) [5], and Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (GC–MS) [6]. Although these methods offer high accuracy, complexity, expensive equipment, and stringent personnel requirements render them unsuitable for rapid SA detection. Consequently, there is a crucial need for the development of a simple, fast, and economical method for SA analysis.

Chemiluminescence (CL) is a phenomenon involving light radiation. In comparison to other analytical techniques, CL provides advantages such as a wide linear range, simple equipment, and fast detection speed [7]. Currently, it stands as one of the most effective analytical methods, widely applied in environmental monitoring, clinical diagnosis, food safety, and among other fields [8]. Carbon Dots (CDs) represent a class of carbon nanomaterials with a size smaller than 10 nm [9]. They have low toxicity, excellent water solubility, good biocompatibility, simple preparation, and environmental friendliness [10]. These characteristics have positioned CDs prominently in applications such as drug delivery, nano-probes, catalysis, bioimaging, luminescent devices, and sensing detection [11]. Researchers have conducted thorough studies and achieved significant progress in the synthesis and applications of CDs. Some have introduced CDs into various CL systems, demonstrating their enormous potential as CL probes [12]. Nowadays, CDs utilized in CL are primarily synthesized using chemical precursor materials, like polyethylene glycol, glucose, and citric acid [13]. However, there are limited reported instances of employing biomass precursor materials for CDs synthesis and their subsequent in CL systems.

Aucklandia lappa Decne is a traditional Chinese medicinal herb known for its anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antihypertensive effects [14]. The main medicinal part is the rhizome, and the value of wood incense leaves is often overlooked, resulting in waste. This study utilized discarded Aucklandia lappa Decne leaves as a green precursor material and synthesized water-soluble CDs through a one-step hydrothermal method. Phosphorus (P) and chlorine (Cl) elements were successfully doped into the CDs through element doping. The synthesized P, Cl-CDs significantly enhanced the CL signal of the acidic-KMnO4 system. Upon the addition of salicylic acid (SA), the CL intensity of the P, Cl-CDs-KMnO4 system was markedly suppressed, and the degree of suppression was positively correlated with the concentration of SA. Building on this foundation, a novel method for detecting SA was developed. The method exhibited high sensitivity and exceptional selectivity, rendering it suitable for determining SA content in aspirin tablets with satisfactory results.

The morphology and lattice structure of P, Cl-CDs was visualized through transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images obtained using the Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin field emission transmission electron microscope (FEI, CZ). Elemental composition and functional group types of P, Cl-CDs were analyzed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using the ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). Further characterization was conducted through Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), recorded with the Nicolet iS10 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). X-ray diffraction spectra (XRD) were acquired using the D8 ADVANCE X X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, GER), and defect levels in CDs were assessed through Raman spectra obtained with the Renishaw in Via Raman spectrometer (UK). Fluorescence spectra were acquired using an F-7100 spectrometer from Hitachi Co., Ltd., Tokyo Japan. CL signals were recorded using the IFFM-D flow injection CL analyzer (Xi'an Ruimai Electronic Technology Co., Ltd.), and ultraviolet absorption spectra (UV) were captured with the TU-1810DSPC UV–visible spectrophotometer (Beijing Puxi General Instrument Co., Ltd.). The synthesis of CDs was performed in an electric heating air-blowing drying oven (GZX-9246MBE).

Aucklandia lappa Decne leaves were harvested from the Aucklandia lappa Decne cultivation base in Quanxiu Village, Guanmian Township, Kaizhou District, Chongqing, China. Aspirin was purchased from a local pharmacy. KMnO4 was sourced from Chengdu Cologne Chemical Co., Ltd. Formaldehyde (HCHO) was purchased from Chengdu Cologne Chemical Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (HCl), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), nitric acid (HNO3), and concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4) were purchased from Chongqing Wansheng Chuandong Chemical Co., Ltd. SA was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute. HCl, H2SO4, H3PO4, and HNO3 were all of analytical grade. Deionized water was utilized in the experiments.

The operational setup of the flow injection CL system is illustrated in Fig. 1 . A primary pump, a secondary pump, and two three-way valves are utilized to convey all solutions entering the reaction. Equal flow rates are set for both the main pump and the secondary pump. Solutions of KMnO4, HCl, HCHO, and P, Cl-CDs are transferred into the flow path through the main pump, converging at the three-way valve. Carrier H2O and the SA sample solution are transported via the secondary pump. Following convergence through a Y-shaped tube and the main pump system, the solutions enter the dark box and proceed to the subsequent reaction step in the flow cell. The CL signal is captured by a photomultiplier tube and fed into a computer for data collection. The influence on the relative CL intensity is quantified as ΔI, where ΔI = I0 − I1, with I0 and I1 representing the CL signals in the presence and absence of SA, respectively.

CDs were synthesized via a previously reported one-step hydrothermal method. Weigh 1 g of discarded Aucklandia lappa Decne leaf powder, add 50 ml of distilled water, and subject it to 40 mins of ultrasonication. Following ultrasonication, add 1 ml each of hydrochloric acid (HCl) and phosphoric acid (H3PO4). Transfer the mixed solution to a stainless-steel high-pressure reaction vessel lined with polytetrafluoroethylene, then place the reaction vessel in an electric heating air-blowing drying oven and heat it to 180 °C for 4 h. Once the reaction is complete, remove the reaction vessel and let it cool to room temperature. Retrieve the resulting solution, filter it multiple times, and then dialyze it in a dialysis bag (MW = 500–1000) over 48 h. The obtained product is named P, Cl-CDs and stored at 4℃ in a refrigerator. The synthesis of single-doped CDs (P-CDs and Cl-CDs) and undoped CDs is identical to that of P, Cl-CDs, only with the reduction of the corresponding doping reagents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: QY and CL intensity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface functional group structure of CDs can be modified by doping with heteroatoms to give CDs more significant luminescence properties [15]. In order to find the optimal CDs, the fluorescence quantum yield and the enhancement effect of the four CDs on the acidic KMnO4 CL system were compared. The fluorescence quantum yields are shown in Table S1, in descending order of P, Cl-CDs, Cl-CDs, P-CDs, and CDs. The effect of the four types CDs on the acidic KMnO4 system were shown in Fig S1, the highest enhancement of acidic KMnO4 systems was achieved by P, Cl-CDs, followed by Cl-CDs, P-CDs and CDs, respectively. The results showed that P, Cl-CDs had higher quantum yields and excellent CL properties, so P, Cl-CDs was chosen for the subsequent experiments.

Based on the TEM images (Fig. 2 a), it is evident that P, Cl-CDs exhibit a regular spherical structure with good dispersibility. The particle size of P, Cl-CDs ranges from 1 to 6 nm, with an average diameter of 3.08 nm (the right inset of Fig. 2 a). High-resolution TEM images (the left inset of Fig. 2 a) reveal that P, Cl-CDs possess a crystal structure with lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, closely resembling the (100) diffraction plane of graphite [16]. The XRD curve (Fig. 2 b) shows a diffraction peak for P, Cl-CDs in the 2θ = 20-34° range, consistent with the characteristic diffraction peak of amorphous carbon, and the peak position is similar to the (002) crystal plane of graphite [17]. This proves that P, Cl-CDs have a graphite-like structure. It is noteworthy that the diffraction peak range of P, Cl-CDs is broader and shifted to the right compared to CDs. This is attributed to the doping of P and Cl, which alters the surface functional groups (O, P, Cl groups) of graphene, causing the diffraction peak to shift rightwards and resulting in a wider carbon spacing [18].

Raman spectroscopy results (Fig. 2 c) indicate that P, Cl-CDs showcase two distinct peaks at approximately 1650 cm−1 and 1450 cm−1, representing the crystalline G-band and disordered D-band, respectively. This suggests the coexistence of crystalline and amorphous graphite phases in P, Cl-CDs. Upon fitting the D-band and G-band, the peak area ratios are 48.48 % and 51.51 %, indicating a relatively high degree of graphitization in P, Cl-CDs. (Fig. 2 d) presents the UV spectrum of P, Cl-CDs. The right inset reveals that P, Cl-CDs emit bright blue-white light under UV irradiation (365 nm). The UV–vis spectrum of P, Cl-CDs features a prominent absorption peak at 250 nm and a weaker absorption peak at 350 nm, possibly attributed to the π–π* transitions of CC and the n–π* transitions of CO (see curve a) [19].The fluorescence(FL) spectra (middle inset, Fig. 2 d) of P, Cl-CDs were measured across excitation wavelengths ranging from 310 nm to 420 nm, with the maximum emission located at 414 nm under excitation at 330 nm. The phenomenon of excitation dependent emission may be related to the fact that the different surface states and dimensions affect the bandgap of P, Cl-CDs [20].

XPS provides additional insights into the elemental composition of P, Cl-CDs. The XPS spectra of P, Cl-CDs and CDs (Fig. 3 a), two prominent peaks at 531.1 eV and 285.0 eV are observed, corresponding to O 1 s and C 1 s, respectively. The XPS spectrum of P, Cl-CDs reveals two weaker peaks at 191.0 eV and 133.1 eV, attributed to Cl 2p and P 2p, respectively. This proving the successful doping of P and Cl. The C 1 s spectrum of P, Cl-CDs (Fig. 3 b) reveals four peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV, 288.6 eV, and 287.3 eV, corresponding to the absorption peaks of CC, CO, CO, and CCl, respectively [21]. The O 1 s spectrum shows absorption peaks at 531.9 eV and 530.5 eV, corresponding to CO and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 3 c). The Cl 2p spectrum of P, Cl-CDs shows absorption peaks at 199.6 eV and 198.3 eV, corresponding to Cl 2p 1/2 and Cl 2p 2/3, respectively (Fig. 3 d). Furthermore, the P 2p spectrum of P, Cl-CDs exhibits absorption peaks at 133.7 eV and 132.4 eV, corresponding to the presence of PC and PO bonds [22] (Fig. 3 e). The XPS results confirm the successful doping of P and Cl, resulting in the formation of P, Cl-CDs. This is consistent with the XPS results, suggesting enhancements in surface functional groups, chemical activity, and electronic properties of P, Cl-CDs.

Surface functional groups of P, Cl-CDs and CDs were further investigated using FT-IR. (Fig. 3 f) shows absorption peaks around 2934 cm−1 and 3405 cm−1 in both P, Cl-CDs and CDs, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of CH and OH. Peaks at 1641 cm−1, 1408 cm−1, and 1157 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO, CC, and CO, respectively [23]. Notably, three distinctive peaks at 1259 cm−1, 756 cm−1, and 610 cm−1 appeared in P, Cl-CDs, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of CP, CCl, and PO, respectively [22]. These results verify the successful doping of P and Cl as well as the formation of P, Cl-CDs. This aligns with the XPS results, suggestion enhancements in surface functional groups, chemical activity, and electronic properties of P, Cl-CDs.

The CL system guided by KMnO4 requires an acidic environment, emphasizing the importance of selecting an appropriate acidic medium. While maintaining constant concentrations of KMnO4, HCHO, and P, Cl-CDs constant, the CL intensity was investigated in four acidic media: 1 M HCl, H2SO4, H3PO4, and HNO3. In Fig. S2, it is evident that the CL intensity is maximal when the system is exposed to HCl, as indicated by the results. Fig. S3 presents the CL kinetics curve, obtained from experiments that explored the combination of the new acidic KMnO4 system with P, Cl-CDs. The highest CL signal was observed when P, Cl-CDs was added to the KMnO4 + HCl + HCHO system. Therefore, the primary CL system selected for subsequent study is P, Cl-CDs + KMnO4 + HCl + HCHO.
To optimize conditions for the CL sensor in detecting SA, the influence of KMnO4, HCl, and HCHO concentrations, as well as the dilution factor of P, Cl-CDs on the CL intensity were investigated.

Investigated the impact of P, Cl-CDs within a dilution range of 10 to 100 times on the CL signal (Fig. S4). The CL signal was suppressed when the dilution factor of P, Cl-CDs was below 10 times, attributed to the quenching effect caused by the high concentration of P, Cl-CDs in the system. Within the range of 10 to 50 times, the CL intensity gradually increased. The highest CL intensity was achieved at a dilution factor of 50 times. However, with a further increase in the dilution factor from 50 to 100 times, the CL intensity gradually decreased. Therefore, P, Cl-CDs diluted by a factor of 50 times was chosen for subsequent experiments.

HCl creates the acidic environment for the reaction and is a critical factor affecting CL intensity. The impact of HCl in the range of 0.1 to 1.1 M on CL intensity was investigated. As shown in Fig. S5, the CL intensity increased with the rise in HCl concentration, peaking at 0.9 M. However, further increases in HCl concentration resulted in a decline in CL intensity. Hence, 0.9 M HCl was selected for subsequent research.

KMnO4 serves as the oxidizing agent in this CL sensor, with its concentration being a crucial factor influencing the CL signal. The impact of KMnO4 on CL was studied in the range of 0.1 to 1.1 mM (Fig. S6). As the concentration of KMnO4 increased from 0.1 to 1 mM, the CL signal increased. The highest CL signal was obtained at a KMnO4 concentration of 1 mM. However, when the KMnO4 concentration exceeded 1 mM, the CL signal began to decrease. Therefore, 1 mM KMnO4 was selected as the optimal concentration for the subsequent research.

HCHO can synergistically enhance the CL signal of the CL sensor in conjunction with P, Cl-CDs. Therefore, the influence of HCHO on the CL signal was investigated within the concentration range of 1 % to 9 %. Depicted in Fig. S7, the CL intensity continuously increased as the concentration of HCHO varied from 1 % to 6 %, reaching its peak at 6 % HCHO. Subsequently, with further increases in HCHO concentration, the CL intensity began to decline. Hence, 6 % HCHO was selected as the optimal condition for subsequent experiments.

As shown in Fig. S8, under the optimal experimental conditions, the CL intensity of P, Cl-CDs + KMnO4 + H+ system decreased in the presence of SA. The CL intensity was directly proportional to the SA concentration in the range of 1to 100 μM, and the limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of this method were calculated to be 4.2 nM and 14.1 nM, respectively (LOD = 3 SD/K, LQD = 10 SD/K, where SD is the standard deviation and K is the slope of the linear regression equation). Fig. 4 a display good linearity of CL intensity with the concentration of SA. The linear regression equation, ΔI = 94.853 × 106 X + 895.94, R2 = 0.9976(F = 1223.035, F-Critical = 5.32), in which X is the concentration of Salicylic acid (μM), ΔI = I1–I0, with I0 and I1 representing the CL signals in the presence and absence of SA, respectively. Performing eleven repeated measurements at a concentration of 20 μM SA yielded a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.9 % (n = 11), indicating good reproducibility of the method. The results of different methods for the determination of SA are presented in Table 1 . The new method developed in this study exhibits a border linear range and a lower detection limit compared to other reported detection methods.

Considering potential interference from various ions found in food and pharmaceuticals, which could impact the accuracy and sensitivity of the experiment, validated the anti-interference capability of the P, Cl-CDs CL probe when combined with FI-CL for detecting salicylic acid. Various concentrations of potential interferents were introduced into a 10 μM SA solution, and subsequent CL tests were performed. Fig. 4 b illustrates those substances such as 1000 times Ca2+, Cl−, Na+, OH−, K+, CO3 2−, 500 times HCOO−, NH4 +, Mg2+, SO4 2−, 100 times Al3+, NO3 −, SO3 2−, glucose, and 10 times Fe3+, Cu2+, Sn2+, BrO3 −, C2O4 2−, starch had no interference with the detection of SA. These results demonstrate the robust anti-interference capability and stability of the P, Cl-CDs + KMnO4 + H+system.

To assess the practical applicability, and feasibility of this method, the proposed method was applied for the determination of SA in aspirin tablets with varying shelf-life, and also determined the SA content by HPLC method. The results of the proposed method are presented in Table 2 in which they are compared with the results from the HPLC method to confirm the accuracy of analysis. Student’s t-test revealed no significant difference between added and founded results. The recovery of SA by the standard addition has been tested also to evaluate the veracity of the developed method. The recovery rates ranging from 96.9 % to 104.3 %, confirming the developed method for determination SA in expired aspirin tablet was reliable and feasible.

The CL reaction mechanism of the KMnO4 + HCHO system under acidic conditions has been reported. Two primary mechanisms are responsible for generating CL signals. Firstly, KMnO4 oxidizes HCHO to produce singlet oxygen (1O2) as well as •OH and •O2 −, these two radicals initiate reactive oxygen chain reactions in the aqueous solution, generating excited-state (1O2)2*[30]. Secondly, KMnO4 is reduced to produce a high concentration of Mn(III), leading to the generation of more excited-state Mn(II)*[31]. Eventually, the products of these two excited states revert to the ground state, producing the CL signal (reactions (1)–(2). Upon adding P, Cl-CDs, the CL intensity of the CL system increases by approximately tenfold.

To identify the luminescent species involved in the CL reaction and determine whether 1O2, •OH, and •O2 − in the chemiluminescent reaction, the influence of different free radical scavengers on the CL intensity of the P, Cl-CDs + HCHO + H++KMnO4system, excluding P, Cl-CDs was studied, as shown in Fig. 4 b. Ascorbic acid (AA), widely used as a scavenger for reactive oxygen and hydroxyl radicals [32]. Adding 1 mM AA resulted in a 68.7 % and 47.9 % decrease in CL intensity in the existence and nonexistence of P, Cl-CDs, respectively, indicating the involvement of the free radical pathway in CL emission. NaN3 is an effective quencher for 1O2 through physical quenching [33]. The addition of 1 mM NaN3 led to a 23.6 % and 35.6 % suppression of CL intensity in the nonexistence and existence of P, Cl-CDs, respectively. Thiourea (TU) is effective in scavenging •OH at low concentrations [34]. Adding 1 mM TU resulted in approximately 12.5 % and 19.4 % suppression of CL intensity. NBT is a scavenger for •O2 − [35]. The addition of 1 mM NBT resulted in a 11.7 % and 19.5 % decrease in CL intensity in the nonexistence and existence of P, Cl-CDs, respectively. The above results show that 1O2, •OH, and •O2 − were produced in the system. And when P, Cl-CDs was added, more free radicals were generated which resulted in stronger CL intensity. Thus, P, Cl-CDs acted as a catalyst in the system to accelerate the reaction rate.

The role of P, Cl-CDs in the system was explored using UV–vis spectroscopy. As depicted in (Fig. 4 d), the characteristic absorption peak of KMnO4 (curve an at 540 nm) disappeared after the addition of P, Cl-CDs. The absorption peak of P, Cl-CDs at around 260 nm decreased gradually, while the peak at around 350 nm disappeared (curve c). This suggests that P, Cl-CDs not only acts as a catalyst to catalyze the reaction of KMnO4 but also participates in the system's reaction. Due to the unique properties of P, Cl-CDs, •O2 − and •OH produced in the acidic KMnO4 system can inject electrons and holes into the P, Cl-CDs, respectively, resulted in the formation of P/Cl-CDs•+ and P, Cl-CDs•−, and subsequent electron transfer between P, Cl-CDs•+ and P, Cl-CDs•− annihilates to form P, Cl-CDs* [36]. It is well known that singlet oxygen has different wavelengths in the emission spectrum due to the different transitions that occur in the ground state, the singlet oxygen state, and the three molecular pairs comprising the two states [37–39]. Chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CRET) between (1O2)2* (energy donor) and P, Cl-CDs (energy acceptor) is possible due to the overlap of the absorption spectra of P, Cl-CDs and (1O2)2* and the good optical properties of P, Cl-CDs [40–42].

The addition of SA produces quenching effect on the system, in order to gain insight into the mechanism of inhibition of CL signals by SA, the effect of different radical scavengers on CL signals in the presence of different concentrations of SA was evaluated, as shown in Table S2.Compared with the case in the absence of SA, the inhibition effect of NBT and NaN3 on the system remains apparent at an SA concentration of 10 μM, while that of TU is weakened, when the concentration of SA at 100 μM, the TU basically appears to have no inhibition of CL signals of the system, which proves that the presence of SA reduces the number of •OH, this is the cause of the inhibition. As shown in Fig. 4.d (curve d), upon adding salicylic acid, the positions of the absorption peaks in the system remain unaltered, however the characteristic absorption peaks of SA and KMnO4 vanished. The Fig. 4.d (curve e) indicates that the characteristic absorption of SA and KMnO4 vanished when SA and KMnO4 were co-existed [43], confirming the occurrence of a reaction between KMnO4 and SA. It is noteworthy that when added SA to KMnO4, it was found that the solution rapidly changed colorless, indicating a rapid reaction between KMnO4 and SA. This reaction is the one of causes of the inhibition of CL emission by SA.

Based on the aforementioned results, it is believed that three luminescent species are present in the whole system after the addition of P, Cl-CDs: the excited states Mn (II)*, (1O2)2* and P, Cl-CDs*. Furthermore, P, Cl-CDs assumes two roles in the reaction process: (1) Acting as a catalyst, P, Cl-CDs accelerate the entire reaction process, generating more free radicals and leading to the production of additional excited states of (1O2)2* and Mn (II)* (reaction (3) [44], (2) Luminescent species: possessing good luminescent properties, P, Cl-CDs serve as an energy acceptor in CRET, obtaining energy from (1O2)2*, forming P, Cl-CDs* (reaction (4). Meanwhile, P, Cl-CDs receives electron and hole injections from •OH and •O2 − and subsequent electron transfer annihilation to form the P, Cl-CDs*(reaction (5)&(6). Eventually, Mn (II)*, (1O2)2* and P, Cl-CDs* revert to the ground state, releasing energy and generating light radiation, thus forming the CL signal (reaction (7). The addition of SA inhibits the CL signal in the system, firstly, SA reacts with •OH, producing two adducts: 2,3-dihydroxybenzoic acid (2,3-DHBA) and 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid (2,5-DHBA) [45], resulting in a reduction in the yield of P, Cl-CDs* and (1O2)2*. Secondly, SA and P, Cl-CDs compete with KMnO4, reducing the oxidant for the entire reaction, leading to a decrease in the CL intensity. Based on the above studies, the emission mechanism of CL is summarized in scheme 1 .
(1) KMnO4 + HCHO + H+→Mn(III)+•OH +•O2 −+(1O2)2*
(2) Mn(III) → Mn(II)*
(3)
P ,
Cl
- CDs +
KMnO 4
+ HCHO +
H
+
→
Catalytic
1
O 2
2 ∗
+ Mn
(II) ∗
(4)
1
O
2
2
∗
+
P ,
Cl - CDs
→
CRET
P ,
Cl -
CDs ∗
+ 2
O
2
(5)
•O2 −+ •OH + P, Cl-CDs → P, Cl-CDs•++ P, Cl-CDs•−
(6) P, Cl-CDs•++ P, Cl-CDs•−→P, Cl-CDs*
(7) P, Cl-CDs*+Mn (II)*+(1O2)2*→P, Cl-CDs + Mn (II) + O2 + hv

In this study, utilized discarded Aucklandia lappa Decne leaves as precursor materials and employed a one-step hydrothermal method for synthesis of P, Cl-CDs with high CL performance and good dispersibility. P, Cl-CDs significantly enhanced the CL activity of the acidic potassium permanganate system, establishing a novel P, Cl-CDs-H+-KMnO4 CL system. This study shown that P, Cl-CDs acted as high-efficient CL catalysts and luminescent species during the reaction to enhance the CL intensity. Additionally, the inhibitory effect of salicylic acid (SA) on the P, Cl-CDs-KMnO4-H+ CL system led to the development of a simple and cost-effective CL probe for the analysis of free SA in expired aspirin. This discovery lays the foundation for developing economical, reusable, and fast-detecting SA chemiluminescent probes, offering new perspectives for the analytical applications of Chemiluminescence-CDs in food and drug research.

Yujie Xiao: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xian Yang: Investigation. Xuan Tan: Methodology. Yue Zhao: Methodology, Investigation. Ke Cheng: Methodology, Investigation. Hongping Han: Methodology, Investigation. Luoyilan Zhang: Methodology, Investigation. Hao Xue: Methodology, Investigation. Jing Xiang: Methodology, Investigation. Xue Zhang: Supervision, Software. Hong Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by the Science and Technology Research Program of the Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (Grant No. KJZD-K202300504) and the Innovation and Central Nervous System Drug Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (Grant No. 230041-01SZ).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110598.

ce:section.ce:para:
